summary: I am a citizen of the United States and France, and my family has a deep connection to the US. My grandfather, Billy Dean, was born and raised in Chicago, and my grandmother and father also lived in the US at various points. My dad and his sister went to Colorado State University in Fort Collins, Colorado in the 1980s, and my dad lived in the US until 2009 before moving back to Geneva. The US has always been a significant part of my family's history and identity.

France has been a central part of my family's story, especially since my grandmother, Yvonne Rosand, is French. My grandfather moved to France in the 1950s for work, and they lived there before eventually buying a farmhouse near Geneva. I moved to France with my sister when I was nine to live with my dad, and although I struggled with the culture and language at first, France broadened my worldview. Having French citizenship has given me many advantages and a strong connection to Europe.

My grandfather, Billy Roe Dean, was born and raised in Chicago as one of seven siblings. After his father died when he was 15, he worked odd jobs to support his family before enlisting in the United States Marine Corps where he worked in radio and communications. After his service and university, he worked at the Radio Corporation of America and moved to Europe in the 1950s. He met my grandmother, Yvonne Rosand, in France, and together they lived in the US, France, and Switzerland, shaping our family's transatlantic history.

Chicago is where my grandfather, Billy Dean, was born and raised. He grew up there with six siblings, the son of an Irish immigrant who worked on the railroads and a mother from Indiana. Chicago was the starting point of his life before he joined the Marines and eventually moved to Europe. It holds a foundational place in our family history.

Indiana is important to my family because my grandfather's mother was a farm girl from there. Although we didn't live in Indiana, it was part of the roots that shaped my grandfather's upbringing in Chicago. It represents part of the American Midwest heritage in our family story.

My grandfather enlisted in the United States Marine Corps after his father passed away and he had worked many odd jobs. While serving, he worked in radio and communications, which set the stage for his later career at the Radio Corporation of America. His time in the Marines was a pivotal chapter in his life and helped him build skills that shaped his future.

After serving in the Marine Corps and attending university, my grandfather worked at the Radio Corporation of America. This job eventually led him to move to Europe in the 1950s, where he met my grandmother in France. RCA was a key part of his professional life and the reason our family began its long history of living between the US and Europe.

Yvonne Louise Alphonsine Rosand, whom we call Baba, is my grandmother and a French woman whom my grandfather met in France in the 1950s. She and my grandfather married and moved around a lot for work, living in the US, France, and eventually settling near Geneva, Switzerland. Baba is central to our family's European roots and dual citizenship story.

Geneva is where my grandparents eventually bought a farmhouse and where my dad grew up. It became a home base for our family after years of moving between the US and France. My dad moved back to Geneva in 2009, and later my sister and I moved to France nearby to live with him. Geneva represents a significant place in our family's European life.

Switzerland, specifically the area around Geneva, became a home for my family when my grandparents bought a farmhouse there. My dad grew up in Switzerland, and after living in the US for many years, he moved back to Geneva in 2009. Switzerland is part of the European chapter of our family's story and reflects our transnational lifestyle.

Fort Collins, Colorado is where my dad and his older sister went to college in the 1980s at Colorado State University. It was an important place for their education and early adult life in the United States. Although our family has moved around a lot, Fort Collins represents a key American location in our family's journey.

Colorado State University in Fort Collins is where my dad and his sister attended college in the 1980s. It was a significant part of their lives before my dad eventually moved back to Europe. The university marks an important educational milestone for our family in the US.

While living in France as a child, many of my friends were British, American, or related to me. The presence of British friends made the cultural environment diverse but also highlighted my feeling of being 'the American.' The United Kingdom played a subtle role in my social life during that challenging time of transition abroad.
